# Overlay figures v2 — design note

**Generated by** `analysis/scripts/generate_overlay_figures.py` on 2026-04-23.
**Outputs** `maps/article/overlay_{calgary,airdrie,lethbridge,reddeer}_v2.png`.

## Why v2 exists

The v1 overlay was a set-arithmetic Venn — orange for
"majority-only territory", blue for "minority-only territory", brown for
"both plans cover this". The trouble: majority and minority 2026 agree on
the **exterior footprint** of every region (each plan partitions all of
Alberta, so the outside-envelope union is the entire province), and they
mostly agree on the **interior boundaries** as well (80-plus EDs are
identity renames). The disagreement is concentrated in how a short list of
urban-adjacent districts is drawn and named. On a set-arithmetic overlay
that boils out as near-universal brown — and the most-critical Red Deer
map became a featureless brown rectangle.

v2 abandons set arithmetic in favour of a **side-by-side "show both maps"**
layout. The left panel is the majority's districts for the region; the
right panel is the minority's. Both panels share extent, projection
(EPSG:3401), and scale; the 2–4 districts the article flags are
colour-filled against a neutral ivory base and labelled inline.

## Layout

- **Figure size** 1600 × 1200 px at 300 DPI (5.33 × 4.00 in). The task
  brief said 1600 × 900 but that crushed each panel into a 2.2 × 1.4 in
  landscape strip; 4:3 was the smallest aspect that kept each panel near
  square for the Calgary / Lethbridge frames.
- **Two panels,** `(0.03 → 0.49)` and `(0.51 → 0.97)` wide, vertically
  `(0.09 → 0.84)` — leaving a top band for the figure title and a bottom
  band for the credit.
- **Fonts:** Playfair Display and Source Sans 3 were specified but are
  not installed on the Windows host. Matplotlib falls back silently; to
  keep the panels consistent with the cover, I use Georgia (serif) for
  the figure title and panel titles, and DejaVu Sans (humanist sans) for
  ED labels. These are the same substitutions `build_cover.py` would
  receive on this machine because Chrome renders the cover via HTML with
  Google Fonts but matplotlib does not download Google Fonts.
- **Palette:**
  - Urban base fill `#f0e4d0` (warm ivory), rural base `#dcd4c4` (cool
    ecru). A polygon's area decides which base to use (threshold
    2000 km²). This is editorial, not semantic; the goal is a subtle
    visual break between city cores and rural surrounds.
  - Highlight orange `#e87722` (majority), blue `#335c81` (minority) —
    matching the cover palette.
  - Proxied-from-2019 polygons get a warm-brown `///` hatch so the
    reader sees at a glance which tiles are approximate Tier-C shapes.
- **Scale bar** on the left panel only; **north arrow** on the right
  panel only. Each placement preserves readable map area.
- **City marker** inside both panels so the reader orients fast.
- **No baked-in caption** — the article's figure caption handles the
  explanation. Only a 2-line credit at bottom right:
  `"Approximate 2026 geometry pending shapefile release. Dimmed polygons in majority panel = 2019 shape used as Tier-C proxy."`

## Data sources

Layered fallthrough, highest-confidence first:

1. `data/v0_1_derived_v7_{majority,minority}_2026_eds.gpkg` — the full
   89-ED partition. Many Tier-C EDs have null geometries when the v7
   script could not transcribe a commission thumbnail; those skip to
   step 2.
2. `data/v0_1_approximate_majority_2026_eds.gpkg` (Tier A+B majority) or
   `data/v0_1_refined_v6_minority_2026_eds.gpkg` (refined Tier-B/C
   minority) as the second primary source.
3. `data/v0_1_{majority,minority}_full_crosswalk.csv` — 88-row
   2019↔2026 ED-name crosswalk. Any 2026 ED name with no polygon in
   steps 1–2 pulls its 2019 parent polygon(s) from the shapefile
   `data/alberta_2019_eds/EDS_ENACTED_BILL33_15DEC2017.shp` and unions
   them; the row is flagged `is_proxy=True` and rendered with the
   hatch pattern.

The two primary paths get us most of the way; the proxy step covers
about 20 majority EDs and 5 minority EDs. The v7 minority gpkg carries
polygons for the hybrid names the article calls out
(`Calgary-Foothills-Airdrie West`, `Calgary-Peigan-Chestermere`,
`Lethbridge-Fort MacLeod-Crowsnest Pass`, the four
`Red Deer-*` hybrids) which the v6 file did not. That is why v7 is the
preferred primary source even though the task directive named v6.

## Region-specific choices

**Calgary** — half-width 42 km, centred on the City Hall (-114.072,
51.045). Majority highlights: Airdrie-West, Chestermere-Strathmore,
Calgary-West-Elbow Valley, Calgary-Glenmore-Tsuut'ina. Minority
highlights: Calgary-Airdrie, Calgary-West-Tsuut'ina,
Calgary-Peigan-Chestermere, Calgary-De Winton. Calgary-Airdrie and
Calgary-West-Tsuut'ina are proxied from 2019 (`Airdrie-Cochrane` and
`Calgary-West` respectively); these are flagged in the rendering via
the hatch.

**Airdrie** — half-width 26 km, centred on Airdrie (-114.017, 51.292).
Majority shows the 2-way split (Airdrie-East, Airdrie-West, both
proxied from 2019). Minority shows the 4-way carve: Airdrie East (the
only Airdrie-named minority ED), Calgary-Foothills-Airdrie West,
Olds-Three Hills-Didsbury, Chestermere-Strathmore.

**Caveat:** the article body lists *Calgary-Peigan-Chestermere* as a
fourth minority district touching Airdrie. In the v7 geometry this
district does not actually extend north to Airdrie — it sits south of
Calgary, between Fish Creek and the Peigan First Nation. The fourth
district that does touch Airdrie is Chestermere-Strathmore, which
inherits 2019 identity in the minority plan. I substituted that for
visual accuracy and flagged the swap here.

**Lethbridge** — half-width 75 km, re-centred on (-113.500, 49.750) so
Lethbridge-Fort MacLeod-Crowsnest Pass (which extends west to the BC
border) fits in frame alongside Lethbridge-Cardston (which reaches
nearly to Medicine Hat) and Lethbridge-Little Bow (which sits east of
the city). Majority panel is mostly Tier-C proxies — the only
non-proxied polygons in frame are Livingstone-Macleod and
Medicine Hat-Cypress. Minority panel shows three Lethbridge-prefixed
hybrids.

**Caveat:** the article body also names *Lethbridge-Taber-Warner*. In
the v7 minority partition, Taber-Warner (the 2019 rural ED) is folded
into `Lethbridge-Little Bow`, so only three Lethbridge-named minority
hybrids exist as distinct polygons. The four-way description in the
article remains true at the level of *"how many 2019 rural EDs got
absorbed into Lethbridge-named districts"* — the minority map pulls
territory from Lethbridge-East, Lethbridge-West, Taber-Warner,
Livingstone-Macleod, and Cardston-Siksika into three new
Lethbridge-named hybrids. The map shows the three districts that
result, which is the geographically honest representation.

**Red Deer** — **Option 1 taken.** The two-panel shows Red Deer's 2-way
majority split (Red Deer-North, Red Deer-South, plus the surrounding
rural Lacombe-Clearwater and Sylvan Lake-Innisfail) alongside the 4-way
minority carve (Red Deer-Sylvan Lake, Red Deer-Innisfail,
Red Deer-Blackfalds, Red Deer-Lacombe). The minority's four Red
Deer-prefixed hybrids have v7-derived polygons with coherent boundaries
— they render cleanly at this scale. Option 2 (dropping the panel and
removing the Red Deer reference from the article) was therefore not
needed; the Red Deer narrative survives as a visual.

## Honest caveats

1. **Tier-C approximation visibility.** The hatch pattern makes
   proxied-from-2019 polygons visible but it does not scream. A reader
   who glances past the credit line may not notice that
   Lethbridge-East / Lethbridge-West in the majority panel are 2019
   shapes rather than 2026 Tier-C shapes. The credit explicitly says
   so; the hatch is the visual cue.
2. **Suspended disbelief on rural boundary detail.** At 75 km half-width
   for Lethbridge, the city of Lethbridge is a 10-km smudge in the
   centre of the panel. You can see that the majority has two compact
   city districts and the minority has three regional hybrids; you
   cannot read the fine partition lines at this zoom. That's a
   deliberate trade — the article's argument about Lethbridge is a
   regional one (are rural voters being drawn into Lethbridge-named
   districts?), so the wide frame serves the argument better than a
   city-only zoom would.
3. **Airdrie / Lethbridge naming differences in v7.** "Airdrie East"
   (no hyphen) in the minority gpkg vs. "Airdrie-East" (hyphen) in the
   majority gpkg is a real data artefact and not a typo in the script.
   Propagating either spelling would misrepresent the gpkg; the script
   passes them through as-stored.
4. **Font fidelity.** Playfair Display and Source Sans 3 are
   downloaded/rendered only by Chrome when `build_cover.py` makes the
   cover; matplotlib running these panels falls back to Georgia and
   DejaVu Sans. If exact typeface matching is needed, the fonts would
   need to be installed into the Windows font directory and the
   matplotlib font cache rebuilt.

## How to regenerate

```
PYTHONIOENCODING=utf-8 python analysis/scripts/generate_overlay_figures.py
```

Takes about 10 seconds on the reference machine.

- `analysis/scripts/generate_overlay_figures.py` — new script
- `analysis/methodology/overlay_figures_v2.md` — this note

The v1 artefacts remain in place: `analysis/scripts/build_overlay_figures.py`,

`maps/article/overlay_{calgary,airdrie,lethbridge,reddeer}.png` (without
